schools.summary: The weight of the adjuration: 'I charge (diamartyromai — solemnly testify, adjure) thee therefore before (in the sight and presence of) God, and the Lord Jesus Christ' — Paul laying his charge on Timothy in the most solemn manner, as in the very presence of God and Christ; 'who shall judge the quick (the living) and the dead' — Christ the universal Judge of all, before whom Timothy must finally answer; 'at (and by) his appearing (epiphaneia — his glorious second coming) and his kingdom' — the charge reinforced by the prospect of Christ's appearing and the kingdom he will establish (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: I charge thee therefore before God, and the Lord Jesus Christ
who shall judge the quick and the dead at his appearing and his kingdom
the solemn charge before God and Christ the Judge
adjured before the coming Judge

schools.summary: The heart of the charge: 'preach (keryxon — herald, proclaim as a herald) the word (the gospel, the message of God)' — the supreme duty of the minister; 'be instant (epistethi — be ready, stand by, be urgent, persist) in season, out of season (eukairos akairos — when convenient and when inconvenient, at all times)' — ever ready to preach whether the occasion seems favourable or not; 'reprove (convict, refute error), rebuke (censure sin), exhort (encourage to duty) with all longsuffering (patience) and doctrine (teaching)' — the varied work of preaching done patiently and with sound instruction (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: Preach the word; be instant in season, out of season
reprove, rebuke, exhort with all longsuffering and doctrine
the great charge to preach the word
ready in season and out of season

schools.summary: The urgency behind the charge to preach: 'for the time will come when they will not endure (bear, tolerate) sound (hygiainouses — healthful, wholesome) doctrine' — a season coming when men refuse the wholesome teaching of the gospel; 'but after their own lusts (desires) shall they heap (episoreusousin — accumulate, pile up) to themselves teachers' — instead of submitting to sound teaching, gathering a multitude of teachers who flatter their desires; 'having itching ears (knethomenoi — ears that crave novelty and flattery, needing to be tickled, never satisfied)' — restless ears itching for new and pleasing things, demanding teachers who gratify rather than correct (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Wesley).

themes: the time will come when they will not endure sound doctrine
after their own lusts shall they heap to themselves teachers, having itching ears
the coming refusal of sound doctrine
itching ears craving flattery

schools.summary: The result of itching ears: 'and they shall turn away (apostrepsousin — turn aside, avert) their ears from the truth, and shall be turned (ektrapesontai — be turned aside, diverted) unto fables (mythous — myths, fictions, idle tales)' — they reject the truth and embrace fiction, a willful aversion deliberately refusing the truth that corrects them; the ear that itches for flattery shutting itself against truth and falling into myth and error, for having rejected the truth they fall not into neutrality but into error (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: And they shall turn away their ears from the truth, and shall be turned unto fables
the turning from truth to fables
willful aversion to truth
the alternative to truth is error

schools.summary: The fourfold charge over against the coming apostasy: 'watch (nephe — be sober, be vigilant, keep clear-headed) thou in all things' — watchful and self-possessed in everything, alert against error and danger; 'endure afflictions (kakopatheson — suffer hardship, bear suffering)' — bearing the sufferings of the ministry; 'do the work of an evangelist (a preacher of the gospel, a bringer of good news)' — fulfilling the evangelist's task of proclaiming Christ; 'make full proof of (plerophoreson — fully discharge, completely fulfil) thy ministry' — performing his ministry completely, leaving nothing undone (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: But watch thou in all things, endure afflictions, do the work of an evangelist, make full proof of thy ministry
the fourfold charge: watch, endure, evangelize, fulfil
vigilant and fully faithful
the full discharge of the ministry

schools.summary: Paul's serene view of his approaching death: 'for I am now ready to be offered (spendomai — being poured out as a drink-offering, a libation)' — he sees his coming martyrdom as a sacrifice, his lifeblood poured out as a libation upon the altar, death as an offering to God; 'and the time of my departure (analysis — loosing, as a ship from its moorings or the striking of a tent) is at hand (has come, is present)' — his death imminent, spoken of not as an end but as a departure, a loosing to set sail or a breaking of camp; the aged apostle facing death with calm, even joy (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: For I am now ready to be offered, and the time of my departure is at hand
Paul ready to be offered, his departure at hand
martyrdom as a drink-offering
death as a departure

schools.summary: Paul's threefold triumphant review: 'I have fought (egonismai — contended, striven) a good fight (ton kalon agona — the good contest)' — his whole life a noble contest well fought (the wrestling-match, the boxing, or the spiritual warfare); 'I have finished (teteleka — completed, run to the end) my course (dromon — the race-course, the appointed track)' — the race of his life and ministry run to its appointed end; 'I have kept (tetereka — guarded, preserved) the faith' — whether the body of Christian truth (the deposit, kept pure) or his own fidelity and pledged loyalty (kept unbroken, as a soldier keeps his oath), holding fast the faith to the end (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: I have fought a good fight, I have finished my course, I have kept the faith
Paul's threefold triumphant review
the contest, the race, the faith
a faithful life complete

schools.summary: Paul's certain hope: 'henceforth (for the rest, from now on) there is laid up (reserved, stored up) for me a crown (stephanos — the victor's wreath) of righteousness' — the reward awaiting him, a crown that is the gift and fruit of righteousness, reserved in heaven; 'which the Lord, the righteous judge (who judges justly), shall give (award) me at that day (the day of his appearing and judgment)' — Christ the just Judge awarding the crown, the reward sure because the Judge is righteous; 'and not to me only, but unto all them also that love (have loved and long for) his appearing' — the crown the portion of all who love and look for Christ's return (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Wesley).

themes: there is laid up for me a crown of righteousness, which the Lord, the righteous judge, shall give me at that day
unto all them also that love his appearing
the crown of righteousness laid up
for all who love Christ's appearing

schools.summary: The sudden, personal turn from the heights of the crown to a tender request: 'do thy diligence (spoudason — make haste, give diligence, do your best) to come shortly (quickly, soon) unto me' — the aged, lonely apostle urgently longing for Timothy's company before his death; even the great apostle, serene before martyrdom, feels the natural longing for a friend's presence in his last days, the request revealing the warmth of his heart (Barnes, Robertson).

Chrysostom (with Henry): Paul, facing death and largely forsaken (vv.10-11, 16), yearns for his beloved son to come; the urgency ('shortly,' and again 'before winter,' v.21) reflects both his loneliness and the shortness of the time left to him.

themes: Do thy diligence to come shortly unto me
Paul's urgent request that Timothy come quickly
the lonely apostle's longing
human tenderness before death

schools.summary: The sad report: 'for Demas (once Paul's fellow-labourer, Colossians 4:14; Philemon 24) hath forsaken (abandoned, deserted) me, having loved this present world (the present age, its ease and safety), and is departed unto Thessalonica' — Demas, from love of the world and fear of danger, abandoning the imprisoned apostle (not necessarily open apostasy from the faith, but worldliness drawing him away in Paul's peril); 'Crescens to Galatia, Titus unto Dalmatia' — these two gone, not necessarily in desertion like Demas but perhaps sent or gone on ministry to other regions, so that Paul was left increasingly alone (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Demas hath forsaken me, having loved this present world
Crescens to Galatia, Titus unto Dalmatia
the desertion of Demas and dispersal of companions
worldliness the cause of unfaithfulness

schools.summary: The touching note: 'only Luke (the beloved physician, the evangelist) is with me' — of all his companions, only Luke remained at Paul's side in his imprisonment, the faithful friend who stayed to the end; 'take Mark (John Mark), and bring him with thee' — the same Mark who had once departed from Paul, causing the sharp contention with Barnabas (Acts 15:37-39), now fully reconciled and desired, a beautiful token of restored fellowship; 'for he is profitable (useful, serviceable) to me for the ministry (for service, ministering help)' — the once-rejected Mark now valued as useful for the work (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: Only Luke is with me. Take Mark, and bring him with thee
for he is profitable to me for the ministry
Luke alone with Paul, and the restored Mark
faithful and restored companions

schools.summary: The brief item: 'and Tychicus (Paul's trusted companion and frequent messenger, Ephesians 6:21; Colossians 4:7, a beloved brother and faithful minister) have I sent to Ephesus' — Tychicus, another of Paul's faithful helpers, sent on an errand to Ephesus, perhaps to take Timothy's place there (where Timothy was, 1 Timothy 1:3) so as to relieve him and free him to travel to Paul at Rome, fitting Paul's request that Timothy come (v.9) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: And Tychicus have I sent to Ephesus
Tychicus sent to Ephesus
the trusted messenger dispatched
perhaps to free Timothy to come

schools.summary: The homely, touching request: 'the cloke (phelones — a heavy outer travelling-cloak, for warmth against the cold) that I left at Troas with Carpus (a host or friend there), when thou comest, bring with thee' — the aged prisoner, facing a cold dungeon and winter (v.21), asking for his warm cloak; 'and the books (biblia — papyrus rolls), but especially the parchments (membranas — the vellum documents, more durable and valuable)' — perhaps copies of the Scriptures, his own notes, or important documents; even facing death, Paul wanting his books, the small practical requests revealing his human condition in prison and his continued love of study (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: The cloke that I left at Troas with Carpus, when thou comest, bring with thee
and the books, but especially the parchments
Paul's request for cloak, books, and parchments
the touching humanity of the apostle

schools.summary: The warning about a personal enemy: 'Alexander the coppersmith (a metalworker, perhaps the Alexander of 1 Timothy 1:20 or Acts 19:33) did me much evil (showed me much ill, did me many injuries)' — a determined opponent who had done Paul great harm, perhaps testifying against him or stirring up opposition; 'the Lord reward (apodoie — render, repay; some MSS apodosei, 'will reward,' a statement not a wish) him according to his works' — most take this not as a vindictive curse but as a committal of the matter to God's just judgment, Paul not avenging himself but leaving Alexander to the righteous Judge (cf. Romans 12:19), the words expressing not personal malice but a solemn leaving of judgment to the Lord (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: Alexander the coppersmith did me much evil: the Lord reward him according to his works
Alexander's harm and Paul's committal of him to God
leaving vengeance to God
the wrong committed to the righteous Judge

schools.summary: Paul's warning to Timothy: 'of whom be thou ware (phylassou — be on guard against, beware) also' — Timothy too to be on his guard against Alexander, the enemy who harmed Paul possibly harming Timothy or the work; 'for he hath greatly (vehemently, exceedingly) withstood (antesteke — opposed, resisted, set himself against) our words (our preaching, our message, the doctrine we taught)' — Alexander's harm not merely personal but doctrinal, vigorously opposing the gospel Paul preached, an opponent of the truth dangerous to the church as well as to Paul (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Of whom be thou ware also; for he hath greatly withstood our words
the warning to beware of Alexander
an opponent of the gospel
a danger to the church

schools.summary: The lonely scene: 'at my first answer (apologia — my first defence, the first hearing of his trial) no man stood with me (no one supported me, appeared as my advocate or friend), but all men forsook (deserted, abandoned) me' — at his first court appearance every friend deserted him, none daring to stand by one accused before Nero, leaving him to face the tribunal alone; 'I pray God that it may not be laid to their charge (not reckoned against them, forgiven)' — Paul, like Stephen (Acts 7:60) and his Lord (Luke 23:34), praying for the forgiveness of those who deserted him, with no resentment, only intercession (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: At my first answer no man stood with me, but all men forsook me
I pray God that it may not be laid to their charge
Paul forsaken yet praying for his deserters
Christlike forgiveness

schools.summary: The contrast with the deserting men: 'notwithstanding the Lord stood with me (parestе — was present, took his stand beside me), and strengthened (enedynamosen — empowered, gave inward strength to) me' — when every human friend failed, the Lord himself stood by Paul and gave him strength; 'that by me the preaching (the proclamation of the gospel) might be fully known (fully accomplished, made known to the full), and that all the Gentiles might hear' — the Lord strengthening him so that even at his trial the gospel might be proclaimed before the highest tribunal and reach the Gentile world; 'and I was delivered out of the mouth of the lion' — rescued from the immediate danger of death (whether Nero, the peril of destruction, or Satan) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

themes: Notwithstanding the Lord stood with me, and strengthened me
that by me the preaching might be fully known, and that all the Gentiles might hear
the Lord standing with Paul when all forsook him
delivered out of the mouth of the lion

schools.summary: Paul's confident hope, rising from past deliverance (v.17): 'and the Lord shall deliver (rescue) me from every evil work (every evil deed, every attempt of his enemies, or every assault that would harm his soul)' — not necessarily from death, but from every evil that would do him real (spiritual) harm, the Lord keeping him to the end; 'and will preserve (save, bring safely) me unto (into) his heavenly kingdom' — bringing him safely through death into the kingdom of heaven, deliverance now crowned by salvation into glory, death itself the gateway to the kingdom; 'to whom be glory for ever and ever. Amen' — bursting into a doxology of everlasting praise to the Lord who saves him (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: the Lord shall deliver me from every evil work, and will preserve me unto his heavenly kingdom
to whom be glory for ever and ever. Amen
Paul's confidence of final deliverance into the heavenly kingdom
death the gateway to glory

schools.summary: Paul's closing salutations: 'salute (greet) Prisca (Priscilla) and Aquila' — the devoted husband-and-wife team, Paul's old fellow-workers and friends (Acts 18:2-3; Romans 16:3), tentmakers like himself who had risked their lives for him, repeatedly appearing as hospitable believers who hosted a house-church and instructed Apollos (Acts 18:26); 'and the household (family) of Onesiphorus' — the family of the one who had so faithfully refreshed Paul and sought him out at Rome (1:16-18), Paul greeting the household of his loyal friend (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: Salute Prisca and Aquila, and the household of Onesiphorus
the greeting to Prisca, Aquila, and Onesiphorus's household
Paul's faithful fellow-workers
his loyal friends honoured

schools.summary: The personal news Paul sends: 'Erastus (perhaps the city treasurer of Corinth, Romans 16:23, or the companion of Acts 19:22) abode (remained, stayed) at Corinth' — Erastus having stayed behind at Corinth, not come on to Rome; 'but Trophimus (the Ephesian, Acts 20:4; 21:29) have I left at Miletum (Miletus) sick' — Paul obliged to leave his companion behind, ill, at Miletus; the fact that Paul left Trophimus sick (not miraculously healing him) showing that the gift of healing was exercised at God's will, not at the apostle's own pleasure, miracles serving the gospel not personal convenience (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Erastus abode at Corinth: but Trophimus have I left at Miletum sick
the news of Erastus at Corinth and Trophimus left sick
scattered companions
healing exercised at God's will

schools.summary: Paul renews his plea (v.9) with a pressing reason: 'do thy diligence (make haste, do your best) to come before winter' — before the season when sea travel became dangerous or impossible (navigation closed in winter); if Timothy delayed, he might not be able to come, or might come too late, Paul's death being near; 'before winter' carrying both the practical reason (travel, and the cloak wanted against the cold, v.13) and the unspoken one (Paul's approaching death); 'Eubulus greeteth thee, and Pudens, and Linus, and Claudia, and all the brethren' — the few faithful believers at Rome who remained with Paul (Linus by tradition later bishop of Rome) sending their greetings (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).

themes: Do thy diligence to come before winter
Eubulus greeteth thee, and Pudens, and Linus, and Claudia, and all the brethren
the urgent plea to come before winter
greetings from the Roman brethren

schools.summary: The tender final word to Timothy: 'the Lord Jesus Christ be with thy spirit' — Paul's parting prayer that the presence of the Lord Jesus would be with Timothy's inmost soul, the inner man, the personal and intimate blessing prayed into the very heart of his beloved son; 'grace be with you (hymon, plural). Amen' — turning from Timothy to the whole church, Paul closing with his customary benediction, the grace of the Lord Jesus upon all the believers, sealed with 'Amen' — likely the last written words of the apostle (Barnes, Robertson, Gill).

themes: The Lord Jesus Christ be with thy spirit. Grace be with you. Amen
the closing benediction upon Timothy and the church
the Lord with Timothy's spirit
likely Paul's last written words
